content.summary: Always follow Hb with MCV to classify. Hb <8 g/dL = consider transfusion threshold. Symptoms matter — not just the number. Haemodilution in pregnancy lowers Hb (normal ≥10.5 in 3rd trimester).
Mnemonic MALT for microcytic. Normocytic = haemolysis, bleeding, renal disease, early deficiency. MCV alone is not enough — always pair with RDW and clinical context.
MCH closely mirrors MCV. Low MCH + low MCV = classic iron deficiency hypochromic picture. MCH alone rarely changes management but confirms the MCV pattern.
MCHC >36 strongly suggests hereditary spherocytosis — the only common cause. Do osmotic fragility test and blood film. Low MCHC = hypochromic = iron deficiency until proven otherwise.

KEY differentiator: Iron deficiency = HIGH RDW. Thalassaemia trait = NORMAL RDW with low MCV. RDW rises before MCV drops in early iron deficiency — it's the earliest CBC marker of iron deficiency. Use it!
WBC alone is insufficient — always request DIFFERENTIAL. Neutrophils = bacterial. Lymphocytes = viral. Eosinophils = NAACP. Monocytes = TB/fungal/chronic. Basophils = CML.

<0.5 = severe neutropenia — high infection risk, reverse barrier nursing. Left shift (bands / metamyelocytes on film) = active infection. Clozapine: mandatory WBC monitoring. Agranulocytosis = stop drug immediately.
Atypical lymphocytes on film = EBV (mono). Smear cells = CLL. CD4 <200 = AIDS-defining illness. Lymphocytosis + B symptoms + lymphadenopathy = rule out lymphoma urgently.

NAACP mnemonic. >0.5 = eosinophilia requiring investigation. >1.5 = hypereosinophilic syndrome — organ damage risk (cardiac, pulmonary, neurological). Eosinophilia + asthma + vasculitis = EGPA (Churg-Strauss).

<50 = significant bleeding risk. <20 = spontaneous bleed risk. HIT: platelet drop >50% on heparin + thrombosis = STOP heparin, use alternative anticoagulant. TTP = schistocytes + thrombocytopenia + neurological + renal failure + fever.

High retics + anaemia = marrow working but losing RBCs (haemolysis/bleeding). Low retics + anaemia = marrow not responding (aplasia/deficiency). Corrected reticulocyte index <2% = inadequate response = bone marrow problem.

ESR >100 = myeloma, malignancy, serious infection (TB, endocarditis), vasculitis until proven otherwise. Combine with CRP: both ↑ = active inflammation. ESR ↑ + CRP normal = consider myeloma (paraprotein levels, SPEP).

CRP rises and falls FASTER than ESR — the best marker for monitoring treatment response. CRP >100 = bacterial infection or major tissue injury. CRP 5–50 = mild-moderate inflammation, non-specific. Low CRP in suspected SLE flare actually SUPPORTS the diagnosis (SLE doesn't raise CRP unless serositis or secondary infection). Serial CRP every 24–48 hrs is more useful than a single value.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Bacterial infection ★ (rises within 4–6 hrs, peaks 24–72 hrs)
Autoimmune flares (except SLE — see hsCRP card)
SLE flare (paradoxically LOW — use ESR instead) ★

content.summary: hsCRP and standard CRP measure the SAME molecule — difference is only assay SENSITIVITY. Standard CRP detects ≥5 mg/L (infection/inflammation). hsCRP detects 0.1–10 mg/L (cardiovascular risk). FOUR KEY RULES: (1) Use hsCRP for 10-year CVD risk stratification alongside lipids and BP — not for diagnosing infection. (2) If hsCRP >10 mg/L: acute infection/inflammation is present — CANNOT interpret for CV risk, repeat in 2–3 weeks after recovery. (3) JUPITER trial (Ridker 2008): rosuvastatin in patients with LDL <3.4 mmol/L BUT hsCRP >2 mg/L reduced MI/stroke by 44% — established hsCRP as statin therapy target even with normal LDL. (4) hsCRP reflects chronic low-grade inflammation driven by diet, stress, obesity, and physical inactivity — rises years before clinical cardiovascular events. Reynolds Risk Score includes hsCRP for women's CVD risk. Always order on a healthy, infection-free day for CV risk assessment.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: Low CV risk: <1.0 mg/L   |   Intermediate: 1.0–3.0 mg/L   |   High CV risk: >3.0 mg/L   |   Acute inflammation: >10 mg/L (invalidates CV risk use)

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Cardiovascular disease risk ★ (chronic low-grade atherosclerotic inflammation)
Any acute infection or inflammation (spikes >10 mg/L — cannot interpret for CV risk)
Statins ★ (reduce hsCRP independent of LDL — JUPITER trial 2008)

content.summary: ALT is MORE liver-specific than AST. ALT > AST = viral/NAFLD. AST:ALT ≥2:1 = alcoholic hepatitis. ALT >1000 = ischaemic hepatitis, acute viral, or paracetamol toxicity. Always compare to baseline trend.
AST:ALT ≥2:1 = alcoholic hepatitis (De Ritis ratio). Isolated AST rise: check CK — likely muscle source, not liver. Less liver-specific than ALT. Troponin more specific for MI than AST.

Isolated ALP: check GGT. GGT ↑ = liver origin. GGT normal = BONE origin (Paget's, bone mets). ALP + GGT + bilirubin ↑ = cholestatic pattern. Pregnancy ALP = placental isoenzyme — completely physiological, don't investigate.

GGT is the ALCOHOL marker — rises with regular drinking even before liver damage. Confirms liver origin of raised ALP. Normalises 2–6 weeks after stopping alcohol. Very sensitive but non-specific — elevated in many liver conditions.

Visible jaundice >35 µmol/L. Unconjugated ↑ = pre-hepatic (haemolysis) or Gilbert's — urine bilirubin ABSENT. Conjugated ↑ = hepatic or post-hepatic — bilirubin appears in urine (dark urine). Always check direct/indirect split.

Half-life ~20 days — reflects CHRONIC synthetic function. Acute illness lowers albumin non-specifically (negative APP). Used in Child-Pugh score. Check urine ACR if albumin low — could be nephrotic syndrome.

BEST acute synthetic function marker (factor VII t½ = 6 hrs — responds within hours). Prolonged PT that CORRECTS with Vit K = Vit K deficiency (fat malabsorption). Does NOT correct = liver failure. Critical in acute liver failure — drives transplant listing (King's College Criteria).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Liver failure ★ (factors II, VII, IX, X ↓)

content.summary: Non-specific but essential. Haemolysis triad: ↑LDH + ↑unconjugated bilirubin + ↓haptoglobin. Very high LDH in lymphoma = adverse prognosis (IPI score). Also elevated in PE — non-specific marker.

Low haptoglobin = haemolysis until proven otherwise. Consumed binding free Hb in intravascular haemolysis. Interpret with LDH + bilirubin. Absent in neonates — not useful in newborns. Normal haptoglobin does NOT exclude extravascular haemolysis.

Total protein = albumin + globulins. High total protein + low albumin = high globulins → send SPEP/SIFE + serum free light chains (myeloma screen). Globulin gap >4 g/dL is abnormal — always investigate.

Does NOT rise until ~50% nephron loss. Always compare to BASELINE. Trend > single value. Muscle-dependent — a fit 25-year-old male with creatinine 115 is very different from a frail 80-year-old woman. Always check eGFR.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Drugs: cimetidine, trimethoprim (block secretion — not true renal impairment)
Low muscle mass (elderly, cachexia, limb amputation)

content.summary: Urea:Creatinine ratio (µmol:µmol) >100:1 = pre-renal or UPPER GI BLEED. <100:1 = intrinsic or post-renal. Low urea + raised creatinine = liver failure context. In GI bleed: Urea rises disproportionately to creatinine.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Upper GI bleeding ★ (digested blood = protein load)

content.summary: CKD-EPI accounts for age, sex, race, creatinine. Use for CKD STAGING. Drug dose adjustments: metformin stop at G3b; many antibiotics (gentamicin, vancomycin), anticoagulants need adjustment at G3+. eGFR unreliable in AKI — use creatinine trend instead.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: False elevation: low muscle mass (high eGFR despite poor renal function)

content.summary: ASSESS VOLUME STATUS FIRST. Hypovolaemic = diuretics/Addison's. Euvolaemic = SIADH. Hypervolaemic = CCF/cirrhosis/nephrotic. Correct Na slowly (<10 mmol/L per 24 hrs) to avoid central pontine myelinolysis (osmotic demyelination).

K+ >6.5 = CARDIAC EMERGENCY. ECG: peaked T → PR prolongation → wide QRS → sine wave → VF. TREAT: Calcium gluconate FIRST (membrane stabilisation, 10 mins), then insulin+dextrose, salbutamol neb, sodium bicarbonate if acidotic. Dialysis if severe or persistent.

Low HCO₃ → calculate ANION GAP: Na - (Cl + HCO₃). Normal AG = 8–12. High AG (>12) = MUDPILES. Normal AG acidosis = RTA, diarrhoea, saline excess (hyperchloraemic). Use delta-delta ratio to check if mixed disorder.

Refeeding syndrome: severe hypophosphataemia after refeeding a malnourished patient — can cause cardiac arrest and respiratory failure. Monitor electrolytes closely in all refeeding protocols. CKD: high phosphate = vascular calcification risk → dietary restriction + binders.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: DKA treatment (insulin drives Pi into cells)

content.summary: CORRECT for albumin: Adjusted Ca = measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 − albumin). Symptoms: Bones-Groans-Stones-Psychic moans. Ca >3.5 = crisis: rehydrate IV + bisphosphonate. Check PTH to differentiate primary HPT from malignancy (PTH suppressed in malignancy).

Single raised urate does NOT diagnose gout — need clinical features or synovial fluid (negatively birefringent needle crystals under polarised light). Tumour lysis syndrome: rising K + PO₄ + urate + LDH + falling Ca = emergency — rasburicase + aggressive hydration.

Osmolar gap = measured − calculated [2×Na + glucose + urea]. Gap >10 = toxic alcohol (methanol, ethylene glycol, ethanol, propylene glycol). Essential in any unexplained high AG metabolic acidosis. Also calculate to confirm hyperglycaemia as cause of hyperosmolality.

Essential for anion gap calculation. Hyperchloraemia + low HCO₃ + normal AG = hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis → think RTA or diarrhoea or excess saline. Hypochloraemia + high HCO₃ = metabolic alkalosis — think vomiting or diuretics.

ACR >3 mg/mmol = microalbuminuria (early diabetic nephropathy). ACR >30 = macroalbuminuria. Dipstick misses Bence Jones protein — send urine electrophoresis if myeloma suspected. Transient proteinuria: fever, exercise, CCF. Repeat if unexpected positive.

ALWAYS send for MC&S AND cytology if unexpected haematuria. RBC casts = glomerulonephritis (nephritic syndrome). Persistent unexplained haematuria in >40yrs = urology referral (2-week wait, rule out malignancy). Dipstick detects haemoglobin, myoglobin, and free Hb — microscopy needed to confirm RBCs.

Nitrites negative does NOT exclude UTI — Gram-positive organisms (Enterococcus, Staph) don't produce nitrites. Leucocytes alone = non-specific. Sterile pyuria = TB ★, interstitial nephritis, urethritis (chlamydia). Both positive + symptoms = treat as UTI without waiting for culture.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Leucocytes: UTI ★, pyuria, catheter, contamination, interstitial nephritis

content.summary: Glucosuria = blood glucose exceeded renal threshold (normally ~10 mmol/L). Normal urine glucose DOES NOT exclude diabetes — threshold varies. Glucosuria with normal blood glucose = renal tubular defect (Fanconi syndrome — also aminoaciduria, phosphaturia).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Diabetes mellitus ★ (blood glucose >10 mmol/L = renal threshold)

content.summary: In suspected DKA: check urine ketones AND serum beta-hydroxybutyrate (more sensitive/specific). Urine ketones are acetoacetate — serum is BHB. Euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy): ketones elevated with near-normal glucose — easy to miss!

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Type 1 DM: sick day rules — always check

content.summary: Urine bilirubin present = conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia — liver disease or obstruction. Absent bilirubin + high urobilinogen = pre-hepatic jaundice (haemolysis, Gilbert's). Absent both = complete obstruction. Use in conjunction with serum bilirubin fractions.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Urobilinogen ↑: haemolysis ★, hepatocellular disease, early obstruction
Urobilinogen absent: complete biliary obstruction (pale stool + dark urine)

content.summary: SG reflects renal concentrating ability. Fixed SG ~1.010 = isosthenuria = severely impaired concentrating ability (CKD, ATN). Inability to concentrate urine (SG <1.010 despite dehydration) = DI or tubular disease. Urine pH >7 in acidosis = RTA (kidney can't acidify).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: High pH (alkaline) >8: UTI (urea-splitting organisms — Proteus), RTA type 1, vegetarian diet, post-meal
High SG >1.030: dehydration, SIADH, glycosuria, proteinuria
Low pH (acid) <4.5: metabolic acidosis, high protein diet, ketoacidosis
Low SG <1.005: DI ★, psychogenic polydipsia, overhydration

content.summary: ACR preferred over 24-hr urine collection — equally accurate, far more practical. ACR >3 in diabetics = start ACE-i/ARB regardless of BP. Rising ACR in CKD = poor prognosis. Nephrotic syndrome: oedema + hypoalbuminaemia + hyperlipidaemia + massive proteinuria (>3.5g/24hr).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: ACR 3–30: Microalbuminuria (early diabetic nephropathy, cardiovascular risk)

content.summary: Urine casts are formed in renal tubules — always pathological except hyaline casts. RBC casts = active glomerular disease (IgA nephropathy, post-streptococcal GN, vasculitis). Contaminated MSU: >2 organisms, squamous cells on microscopy. Always collect midstream clean-catch sample.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Most common: E.coli ★, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, Proteus

content.summary: Total cholesterol alone is insufficient — always interpret with full lipid profile. Non-HDL cholesterol (TC − HDL) is a better CV risk predictor than LDL alone. Calculate QRISK3 score to guide statin therapy decision.

LDL is the PRIMARY treatment target for cardiovascular risk reduction. Goal depends on risk category. ASCVD/very high risk: LDL <1.4 mmol/L. Primary prevention high risk: <1.8. For FH: genetic testing referral, cascade screening family members.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: <3.0 mmol/L general  |  <1.8 very high risk  |  <1.4 ASCVD

content.summary: HDL is PROTECTIVE — higher is better. Low HDL + high triglycerides = atherogenic dyslipidaemia (classic metabolic syndrome pattern). HDL <1.0 = significant cardiovascular risk. Raising HDL alone (niacin) did not reduce CV events in trials — focus on lowering LDL and treating modifiable risk factors.

TG >10 mmol/L = risk of acute pancreatitis — start fibrates urgently. TG >5.6 = secondary causes must be excluded (DM, hypothyroidism, alcohol, drugs). Fasting sample essential — TG rises significantly after a meal. In metabolic syndrome: high TG + low HDL + central obesity + insulin resistance.

Non-HDL = Total cholesterol − HDL. Includes VLDL, IDL, LDL — all atherogenic particles. Better predictor of CV events than LDL alone, especially in diabetics and hypertriglyceridaemia. Now preferred treatment target in ESC/EAS guidelines.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: <4.0 mmol/L general  |  <2.6 high risk  |  <2.2 very high risk

content.summary: Lp(a) is an independent, inherited CV risk factor. Measure ONCE in lifetime (genetic — doesn't change with lifestyle). High Lp(a) >50 mg/dL = significantly elevated CV risk — consider more aggressive LDL lowering. Statins may slightly increase Lp(a) — important in FH patients.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Specific Lp(a)-lowering agents (inclisiran, pelacarsen — in trials)

content.summary: Two abnormal fasting readings = diabetes (if asymptomatic). One reading + symptoms = sufficient for diagnosis. FPG 6.1–6.9 = impaired fasting glucose (IFG) — intermediate hyperglycaemia, high risk of progression to T2DM. Lifestyle intervention reduces progression by 58%.

HbA1c reflects average glucose over past 2–3 months (RBC lifespan). UNRELIABLE in: haemoglobinopathy, haemolysis, pregnancy, CKD — use fasting glucose or 2-hr OGTT instead. Treatment targets: T1DM = 48–53 mmol/mol. T2DM = 48–58 mmol/mol (individualise — tighter if young, looser if elderly/frail).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Iron deficiency anaemia (falsely elevated — delayed RBC turnover)

content.summary: Gold standard for gestational diabetes screening (GDM). Also used to diagnose IGT when other tests borderline. Patient must eat carbohydrates for 3 days before test. In pregnancy: any GDM = strict glucose targets to prevent macrosomia and complications.

Random glucose ≥11.1 + symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) = diabetes diagnosed without further testing. Random glucose ≥11.1 without symptoms = repeat test needed. Critical values: <2.2 mmol/L = neuroglycopenic symptoms likely; treat urgently.

C-peptide differentiates exogenous insulin abuse (low C-peptide) from insulinoma (high C-peptide). In T1DM: C-peptide confirms beta cell failure — useful when diagnosis uncertain (LADA vs T2DM). During hypoglycaemia: collect serum insulin + C-peptide + glucose simultaneously (Whipple's triad).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Sulphonylurea abuse: high insulin + high C-peptide
Exogenous insulin injection: high insulin + LOW C-peptide (diagnostic)
Type 1 DM: very low/absent C-peptide (destroyed beta cells)

content.summary: BHB is preferred over urine ketones in DKA monitoring (more accurate, faster, bedside). DKA resolution criteria: BHB <0.6 mmol/L + glucose <14 + HCO₃ >18. Euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2i): BHB elevated but glucose near-normal — easy to miss, high index of suspicion needed.

Hypoglycaemia treatment: if conscious = 15–20g fast-acting carb (glucose gel, orange juice, Lucozade) → recheck at 15 mins → repeat if still <4. If unconscious = IV dextrose (10% preferred over 50%) or glucagon IM. In hospital: always identify and treat the CAUSE.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Hyperglycaemia: poor compliance, illness, steroids, intercurrent infection, under-insulinisation
Hypoglycaemia: <4.0 mmol/L = 'four is the floor' — treat. <3.0 = severe. <2.5 = neuroglycopenia

content.summary: ALWAYS correct: Adjusted Ca = measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 − albumin g/L). Symptoms: Bones (pain/fractures), Groans (nausea/constipation), Stones (renal calculi), Psychic moans (depression/confusion/coma). Ca >3.5 = hypercalcaemic crisis: aggressive IV rehydration + IV zoledronic acid. Check PTH to differentiate cause.

Hyperphosphataemia in CKD drives secondary hyperparathyroidism and vascular calcification. Treat with dietary restriction + phosphate binders (sevelamer, calcium carbonate, lanthanum). Hypophosphataemia <0.3 mmol/L = severe — cardiac and respiratory muscle weakness risk.

KEY interpretation: Ca ↑ + PTH ↑ (or inappropriately normal) = PRIMARY hyperparathyroidism. Ca ↑ + PTH ↓ = malignancy or Vit D toxicity. Ca ↓ + PTH ↑ = secondary HPT (CKD/Vit D def). Ca ↓ + PTH ↓ = hypoparathyroidism. PTH independent of Ca = autonomous (tertiary HPT).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Secondary hyperparathyroidism: CKD ★, Vit D def, hypocalcaemia (Ca low, PTH ↑)
Tertiary hyperparathyroidism (autonomous PTH after prolonged 2ary HPT)
Sarcoidosis / Vit D toxicity (PTH suppressed by high Ca)

content.summary: 25-OH Vit D is the storage form — measure this. 1,25-OH (calcitriol) is active form — measure in sarcoid/granulomatous disease (produced ectopically). Deficiency: rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults), proximal myopathy, fatigue, low mood. Replace with colecalciferol (D3). Target 75–100 nmol/L.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Vitamin D deficiency ★ (extremely common — especially South Asia, UK, elderly, veiled)

content.summary: Paget's disease: ALP can be extremely elevated (>1000 U/L) with completely normal GGT — characteristic pattern. Treatment with bisphosphonates normalises ALP. Bone ALP (isoenzyme) can separate bone from liver source if needed. In osteomalacia: ALP ↑ + Vit D ↓ + low phosphate.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Paget's disease of bone ★★★ (very high ALP, normal GGT)

content.summary: T-score compares to young healthy adult peak bone mass. Z-score compares to age-matched peers (use in premenopausal women and men <50). FRAX tool: integrates clinical risk factors + BMD to calculate 10-year fracture risk. Treat if FRAX 10-yr hip fracture risk ≥3% or major osteoporotic fracture ≥20%.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Osteoporosis: postmenopausal women ★, age >70, corticosteroids, hypogonadism, malabsorption, anorexia nervosa, rheumatoid arthritis
Osteopenia: earlier stage, fragility fracture risk increasing

content.summary: Always perform CT head before LP if: papilloedema, focal neurology, GCS <13, immunocompromised, new-onset seizures. IIH: young obese woman, headache, papilloedema, normal MRI — opening pressure >25 cmH₂O diagnostic. Measure in LATERAL DECUBITUS position.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Space-occupying lesion (LP CONTRAINDICATED if raised ICP with SOL)

content.summary: Protein elevated in virtually all serious CNS infections. Albumino-cytological dissociation = high protein + normal cell count = Guillain-Barré ★. Very high protein + sticky/xanthochromic CSF = TB meningitis until proven otherwise. Always pair with cell count and glucose.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: TB meningitis (1–5 g/L, can be very high)
Guillain-Barré syndrome (very high protein, albumino-cytological dissociation)

content.summary: ALWAYS take serum glucose simultaneously. Low CSF glucose = bacteria or TB consuming glucose. Ratio <0.4 = very strong indicator of bacterial meningitis. Normal ratio with high WBC = viral meningitis. Low glucose + high protein + lymphocytes = TB until proven otherwise.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Diabetes mellitus (hyperglycaemia raises CSF glucose proportionally)
Bacterial meningitis ★★★ (<0.4 ratio = strongly suggests bacterial)

_meta.abbr: CSF Glucose — always compare to serum

content.summary: Cell type is THE KEY distinguisher: neutrophils = bacterial (treat immediately — do NOT wait for culture). Lymphocytes = viral or TB. In bacterial meningitis: treat empirically with ceftriaxone + amoxicillin (listeria cover >60yrs, pregnant, immunocompromised) + dexamethasone.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Lymphocytes 10–500: Viral meningitis ★, TB, fungal, aseptic
Eosinophils: parasitic meningitis (angiostrongyliasis), fungal, shunt infection
Mixed: early bacterial, partially treated, listeria, TB

content.summary: If CT head negative but clinical suspicion of SAH: MUST perform LP >2 hrs after symptom onset. Send for spectrophotometry (not just visual inspection). Xanthochromia persists up to 2 weeks. Distinguishing SAH from traumatic tap: SAH = uniformly bloody all tubes, xanthochromia; Traumatic tap = blood clears between tube 1 and tube 4.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Subarachnoid haemorrhage ★★★ (yellow colour from oxyhaemoglobin → bilirubin >2 hrs after bleeding)

content.summary: PSA is NOT a perfect screening test. Sensitivity ~70%, specificity ~60%. Many false positives (prostatitis, BPH) and false negatives. Free:total PSA ratio: <15% = more likely malignant. PSA velocity (rate of rise) and PSA density (PSA/prostate volume) add discriminatory value. Always confirm with MRI and biopsy before treatment decisions.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Recent ejaculation / DRE / catheterisation / prostate biopsy (wait 4–6 wks)
5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) halve PSA — adjust interpretation

content.summary: HCC surveillance: 6-monthly AFP + ultrasound in cirrhotic patients. AFP >400 = likely HCC if liver mass present. Normal AFP does NOT exclude HCC (20% of HCC don't produce AFP). In testicular cancer: AFP never elevated in PURE seminoma — if AFP raised, treat as non-seminomatous. AFP is also tumour marker for gestational trophoblastic disease.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Hepatocellular carcinoma ★ (AFP >400 ng/mL = highly suggestive with liver mass on imaging)
Pregnancy (normal in maternal serum — fetal source)
Neural tube defects (elevated maternal serum AFP)

content.summary: CEA should NOT be used for colorectal cancer screening — it has poor sensitivity for early disease. Main use: POST-RESECTION monitoring for recurrence. Rising CEA after curative resection = recurrence until proven otherwise — CT staging. CEA >10 ng/mL = likely metastatic disease. Normalises after curative surgery.

CA 19-9 is elevated by BILIARY OBSTRUCTION regardless of malignancy — uninformative with jaundice until obstruction relieved. Use to monitor known pancreatic cancer response to treatment. Pre-operatively: CA 19-9 >200 suggests unresectability. Cannot diagnose pancreatic cancer in isolation.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Benign biliary disease (cholangitis, jaundice — can be very high!)
Lewis antigen-negative individuals cannot produce CA 19-9 (5–10% of population — permanently zero)

content.summary: CA 125 alone is not a screening test for ovarian cancer. Use with USS in post-menopausal women with symptoms. RMI (Risk of Malignancy Index) = CA125 × USS score × menopausal status. RMI >200 = refer to gynaecological oncology. CA125 very useful for MONITORING ovarian cancer treatment response and detecting recurrence.

Testicular cancer staging uses AFP + β-hCG + LDH together (S-stage in TNM). Pure seminoma can produce β-hCG (never AFP). Choriocarcinoma: extremely high β-hCG, highly chemotherapy-responsive — do NOT miss. Serial β-hCG is gold standard for monitoring gestational trophoblastic disease treatment response.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Gestational trophoblastic disease ★ (hydatidiform mole, choriocarcinoma — can be very high)
Testicular germ cell tumour ★ (seminoma and non-seminoma)
Some other cancers (liver, gastric, pancreatic — ectopic production)

content.summary: CA 15-3 is used for MONITORING metastatic breast cancer, not for diagnosis or screening. Rising CA 15-3 in known breast cancer = disease progression (confirm with imaging before changing treatment). Up to 25% of breast cancers are CA 15-3 negative. CEA also useful adjunct in breast cancer monitoring.

In lymphoma: LDH is part of the International Prognostic Index (IPI). Elevated LDH = advanced disease, worse prognosis. Serial LDH used to monitor treatment response — falling LDH = responding. In haematology: LDH >2× ULN = high-risk disease marker. Also critical in tumour lysis syndrome monitoring.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Lymphoma ★ (especially aggressive DLBCL — high LDH = poor prognosis, included in IPI score)

content.summary: MUST measure AFP, β-hCG, and LDH BEFORE orchiectomy (staging purpose) and AFTER (half-life assessment). AFP half-life = 5–7 days. β-hCG half-life = 24–36 hrs. Markers not falling appropriately post-op = residual/metastatic disease. Rising markers after completion of chemo = relapse.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: AFP + β-hCG + LDH together = TNM S-staging for testicular GCT
AFP elevated = non-seminomatous component (even if histology shows pure seminoma)
β-hCG elevated: can occur in pure seminoma
Very high markers = poor prognosis group (IGCCCG classification)

content.summary: pH <7.35 = acidaemia. pH >7.45 = alkalaemia. Normal pH does NOT mean normal — compensation can mask mixed disorders. Always interpret pH with PaCO₂ and HCO₃ together. Step 1: Is it acidosis or alkalosis? Step 2: Primary respiratory or metabolic? Step 3: Is compensation adequate?

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation, anxiety, PE, high altitude)
Metabolic acidosis (DKA ★, lactic acidosis, renal failure, diarrhoea)

content.summary: PaCO₂ = respiratory component. ↑ PaCO₂ + ↓ pH = respiratory acidosis. ↓ PaCO₂ + ↑ pH = respiratory alkalosis. Winter's formula for expected compensation in metabolic acidosis: PaCO₂ = (1.5 × HCO₃) + 8 ± 2. If actual PaCO₂ higher than expected = also has respiratory acidosis.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Respiratory acidosis — hypoventilation ★ (COPD, sedation, obesity hypoventilation)
Respiratory alkalosis — hyperventilation ★ (anxiety, pain, PE, pregnancy, mechanical overventilation)

content.summary: P/F ratio (PaO₂/FiO₂): >400 = normal. 200–400 = mild ARDS. 100–200 = moderate ARDS. <100 = severe ARDS. A-a gradient = PAO₂ − PaO₂. Normal A-a = Age/4 + 4 mmHg. Elevated A-a gradient = V/Q mismatch or shunt (not pure hypoventilation). Type 1 RF target SpO₂ 94–98%. Type 2 RF (COPD): target 88–92% (hypoxic drive).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Type 1 RF: PaO₂ <8 kPa + normal/low PaCO₂ (V/Q mismatch — pneumonia, PE, pulmonary oedema, ARDS)
Type 2 RF: PaO₂ <8 kPa + PaCO₂ >6 kPa (hypoventilation — COPD, neuromuscular, obesity)

content.summary: Anion gap (AG) = Na − (Cl + HCO₃). Normal = 8–12 mmol/L. High AG metabolic acidosis mnemonic MUDPILES: Methanol, Uraemia, DKA ★, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis ★, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates. Normal AG (hyperchloraemic) acidosis: diarrhoea ★, RTA, saline overload, acetazolamide.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Respiratory acidosis compensation (chronic CO₂ retention — renal HCO₃ retention)
Metabolic acidosis ★ (DKA, lactic acidosis, diarrhoea, RTA, uraemia, poisoning)

content.summary: Base deficit <−6 in trauma = significant shock — predicts need for massive transfusion. Lactate ≥2 = hyperlactataemia (monitor, investigate cause). Lactate ≥4 = septic shock criterion → 30mL/kg IV fluid + antibiotics within 1 hour (Surviving Sepsis Campaign). Serial lactate clearance (≥10% decrease/2h) guides resuscitation. Persistent high lactate despite fluids = vasopressors + ICU.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Lactate: sepsis/shock ★★, liver failure, metformin toxicity, ischaemia, strenuous exercise

content.summary: INR monitors extrinsic pathway. INR >1.5 = significant coagulopathy. Warfarin reversal: INR 3–5 (no bleed) = hold dose. INR >5 or bleeding = Vitamin K IV + PCC (4-factor) preferred over FFP. Do NOT give FFP routinely for elevated INR — only if active bleeding or pre-procedure. DOAC patients: INR is UNRELIABLE — do not monitor warfarin with DOAC.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Warfarin therapy ★ (target INR 2–3 for AF/DVT; 2.5–3.5 for mechanical valve)
Liver disease ★ (↓ synthesis factors II, VII, IX, X — VII first to fall, shortest half-life)
Hypercoagulable states (not clinically useful as screen)

content.summary: APTT monitors intrinsic pathway. Lupus anticoagulant: APTT prolonged but causes THROMBOSIS not bleeding — mixing study fails to correct (inhibitor). Confirm with DRVVT. LMWH does NOT prolong APTT — monitor with anti-Xa. Haemophilia: isolated prolonged APTT + normal PT + normal bleeding time → factor assay (VIII or IX).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Haemophilia A (Factor VIII ↓) ★ or B (Factor IX ↓)
Lupus anticoagulant ★ (paradox — prolonged APTT but THROMBOSIS risk)

content.summary: D-dimer = RULE-OUT test. Negative D-dimer + low pre-test probability (Wells score) = DVT/PE safely excluded. Age-adjusted cut-off (age × 0.01 µg/mL in >50y) reduces unnecessary CT-PA by 30%. NEVER use D-dimer to diagnose PE — it cannot. Markedly elevated D-dimer (>5) with clinical features = DIC screen: fibrinogen, platelets, PT, APTT, blood film.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Post-surgery, pregnancy, malignancy, sepsis, MI, stroke — all elevate D-dimer

content.summary: Fibrinogen <1.5 g/L = give cryoprecipitate (10 units raises fibrinogen ~1 g/L). Fibrinogen <1.0 g/L = emergency cryoprecipitate. In obstetric haemorrhage: fibrinogen <2.0 = early predictor of severity. DIC diagnosis: ↓ fibrinogen + ↑ D-dimer + ↑ PT + ↑ APTT + ↓ platelets. TT used to detect: heparin contamination, fibrinogen abnormality, dabigatran effect.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Fibrinogen: acute phase response ★, pregnancy, malignancy, post-surgery
TT prolonged: heparin contamination ★ (most common), fibrinogen deficiency, DIC, dabigatran
Reptilase time normal + TT prolonged = heparin effect (reptilase not affected by heparin)

content.summary: Anti-Xa is the CORRECT monitoring test for LMWH (not APTT). Monitor in: extremes of weight (<45kg or >100kg), renal impairment (CrCl <30), pregnancy, neonates. Prophylactic LMWH target: 0.2–0.4 IU/mL. Treatment: 0.6–1.0 IU/mL at 4h post-dose. For UFH infusions: target anti-Xa 0.3–0.7 IU/mL OR APTT ratio 1.5–2.5.

0h/1h algorithm: if hs-cTnT <5 ng/L at 0h AND delta <3 ng/L at 1h = 99.6% NPV for NSTEMI (rule-out). Delta (absolute rise) is MORE important than single value. Peak troponin 12–24h post-MI. Renal failure: use relative delta >20% rather than absolute. Troponin elevation without ACS = 'non-ischaemic myocardial injury' — requires clinical context + ECG + echo.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: hs-cTnI <26 ng/L male, <16 ng/L female | hs-cTnT <14 ng/L

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Type 2 MI (demand ischaemia from PE, sepsis, tachyarrhythmia)
Renal failure (↓ clearance — persistent low elevation)
Very low hs-cTn — MI unlikely if combined with low pre-test probability

content.summary: NT-proBNP rule-in by age: <50y = 450 pg/mL. 50–75y = 900 pg/mL. >75y = 1800 pg/mL. Negative BNP/NT-proBNP has high NPV for HF (~95% — useful to exclude in acute dyspnoea). Obese patients: lower NT-proBNP despite significant HF. Trend is more useful than single value for monitoring treatment response. Persistently elevated despite treatment = poor 12-month prognosis.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: BNP <35 pg/mL (HF unlikely) | NT-proBNP <125 pg/mL (<75y), <300 pg/mL (>75y)

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Heart failure ★★★ (primary indication — both systolic and diastolic)
Obesity (↓ production despite HF — can miss HF in obese patients)

content.summary: CK-MB still useful for: (1) Detecting re-infarction — returns to normal faster than troponin, so new CK-MB rise after normalisation = re-MI. (2) Estimating infarct size (peak correlates with area of necrosis). LDH1 > LDH2 (flipped pattern) = cardiac or haemolytic source. Very high LDH (>1000 U/L) in haematological malignancy correlates with tumour burden. LDH >500 in PCP pneumonia is characteristic.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: CK-MB <25 U/L (<5% total CK) | LDH 135–225 U/L

tables.caption: Causes
rows: CK-MB: MI (rises 4–6h, peaks 12–24h, returns normal 48–72h), cardiac surgery, myocarditis
LDH: MI (late — rises 24–48h, peaks 3–6 days), haemolysis ★, lymphoma, liver disease, PE

content.summary: ANA is a SCREENING test only. Positive ANA → ENA panel: anti-dsDNA (SLE specific ★), anti-Sm (SLE specific), anti-Ro/La (Sjögren, neonatal lupus), anti-Scl-70 (scleroderma), anti-centromere (CREST), anti-Jo-1 (myositis/ILD). ANA pattern: homogeneous = dsDNA/histone. Speckled = Sm/RNP/Ro/La. Centromere = CREST syndrome.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: SLE ★★★ (95–99% sensitive — high sensitivity screening test)
Sjögren syndrome, scleroderma, MCTD, drug-induced lupus
Negative ANA makes SLE very unlikely — high NPV

content.summary: Anti-dsDNA is the MOST USEFUL antibody in SLE monitoring. Serial titres + C3/C4 guide flare prediction. Anti-dsDNA + ↓ complement + active urine sediment = lupus nephritis → kidney biopsy urgently. Anti-Sm is highly specific for SLE (99%) but insensitive — if positive, diagnoses SLE even without meeting full criteria.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: SLE ★★★ (anti-dsDNA: 70% sensitivity, 97% specificity. Anti-Sm: 25–30% sensitivity, 99% specificity)
Anti-dsDNA titres correlate with SLE disease activity — rising = flare, especially nephritis ★
Negative anti-dsDNA — less likely active lupus nephritis

content.summary: Anti-TPO confirms AUTOIMMUNE thyroid aetiology. Positive anti-TPO in euthyroid patient = 4× increased hypothyroidism risk per decade — recheck TSH annually. In pregnancy: positive anti-TPO + TSH >2.5 in T1 = treat with levothyroxine (reduces miscarriage and postpartum thyroiditis risk). Anti-TPO does NOT correlate with severity — it confirms the diagnosis. All patients with hypothyroidism should have anti-TPO checked at diagnosis.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Non-thyroidal autoimmune disease (T1DM, Addison's, pernicious anaemia — associated)
10% healthy euthyroid women (especially >45y) — low-titre, monitor TSH annually

content.summary: Anti-TG critical in thyroid cancer: elevated anti-TG falsely LOWERS serum thyroglobulin (Tg) — making cancer recurrence monitoring unreliable. Always check anti-TG alongside Tg post-thyroidectomy. If anti-TG positive → Tg is unreliable → use imaging (neck USS + whole body scan) instead. TRAb in pregnancy: check at 32–34 weeks in Graves — high TRAb predicts neonatal thyrotoxicosis (plan paediatric review at delivery).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Anti-TG: Hashimoto's (60–70%), Graves' (50%), thyroid cancer follow-up interference ★
TRAb (TSH receptor Ab): Graves' disease ★★★ (95%), neonatal thyrotoxicosis
Negative TRAb effectively rules out Graves' disease

content.summary: ANCA-associated vasculitis (AAV) triad: small vessel vasculitis + ANCA + end-organ damage. Renal emergency: haematuria + proteinuria + crescentic GN + positive ANCA → immunosuppression immediately (rituximab or cyclophosphamide + IV methylprednisolone). Pulmonary-renal syndrome (haemoptysis + GN) = ANCA, anti-GBM, or SLE. Request anti-GBM (Goodpasture) alongside ANCA in pulmonary-renal presentations.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: p-ANCA non-specific: IBD (60% UC), autoimmune hepatitis, drug-induced vasculitis
Negative ANCA makes AAV unlikely (but possible in limited GPA)

content.summary: Anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide) is the most specific test for RA (98%). Anti-CCP + inflammatory polyarthritis = RA until proven otherwise → refer rheumatology, start DMARDs early. High anti-CCP predicts erosive, severe RA — justifies aggressive treatment. RF is non-specific — always pair with anti-CCP. Combined RF + anti-CCP positive = highest specificity for RA (>99%).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: RF also in: Sjögren ★, hepatitis C ★, cryoglobulinaemia, SBE, sarcoidosis, ageing
Anti-CCP: can be positive YEARS before clinical arthritis (pre-RA)
Seronegative RA (20–30% RF/anti-CCP negative — still diagnose clinically + DAS28)

content.summary: Complement pattern interpretation: C3↓ + C4↓ = classic pathway (SLE ★, lupus nephritis, cryoglobulinaemia). C3↓ + C4 normal = alternative pathway (MPGN II, C3 nephritic factor). C4↓ + C3 normal = C1-inhibitor deficiency (hereditary angioedema) or isolated C4 deficiency. Serial C3/C4 useful in SLE monitoring: rising = remission, falling = flare.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Acute phase response — C3 and C4 both rise in infection/inflammation
SLE ★★★ (C3 + C4 both fall — immune complex consumption of classic pathway)
MPGN type II — C3↓, C4 normal (alternative pathway activation via C3 nephritic factor)
Hereditary angioedema — C4 markedly low (C1-inhibitor deficiency)

content.summary: APS diagnosis requires: (1) Clinical: thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity (3+ miscarriages <10wk, 1+ loss >10wk, premature birth <34wk for pre-eclampsia). (2) Lab: positive on 2 occasions ≥12 weeks apart — lupus anticoagulant (LA), anti-cardiolipin IgG/IgM, anti-β₂GP1 IgG/IgM. Triple positivity = highest thrombosis risk. Treatment: warfarin (target INR 2–3) — DOACs INFERIOR to warfarin in APS (rivaroxaban non-inferior for venous only, but NOT for arterial — avoid in arterial APS).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: APS (antiphospholipid syndrome): arterial/venous thrombosis + pregnancy morbidity + positive APLA ★
Transient positivity: infections, drugs — NOT diagnostic

content.summary: Cushing's screening: 1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (normal = AM cortisol <50 nmol/L). OR 24h urine free cortisol. OR late-night salivary cortisol (best test — loss of diurnal nadir). Adrenal insufficiency: AM cortisol <100 nmol/L = likely. 100–500 = inconclusive → Synacthen test (SST): cortisol should rise to >550 nmol/L at 30 min. ACTH localisation: ↑ ACTH = ACTH-dependent (pituitary or ectopic). ↓ ACTH = ACTH-independent (adrenal).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Cortisol ↑: Cushing's ★ (↑ + loss of diurnal rhythm), stress/acute illness, pseudo-Cushing (obesity, depression, alcohol)
ACTH ↑: Cushing's disease (pituitary) ★, ectopic ACTH (SCLC ★ — very high ACTH), Addison's (↑ ACTH compensatory with ↓ cortisol)
ACTH ↓: adrenal adenoma/carcinoma (autonomous cortisol suppresses ACTH), secondary adrenal insufficiency

content.summary: TSH = most sensitive thyroid test. Start with TSH alone. If abnormal → add FT4 (+ FT3 if TSH suppressed). TSH 4–10 mIU/L (subclinical hypothyroid): treat if symptomatic, anti-TPO positive, or pregnant. TSH >10 = treat regardless. Amiodarone: causes hypothyroidism (most common) OR hyperthyroidism — monitor TFTs every 6 months. In pregnancy: TSH >2.5 in T1 → start levothyroxine 25–50 µg/day.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Secondary hypothyroidism (↓ TSH + ↓ FT4 — pituitary failure)

content.summary: PRL >5000 mIU/L = prolactinoma very likely. PRL 1000–5000 = could be drug-induced, hypothyroidism, or macroprolactinaemia. Always exclude: pregnancy + hypothyroidism + medications FIRST. Macroprolactinaemia: PEG precipitation test — >60% precipitates = macroprolactin (clinically insignificant — avoid treatment). Symptomatic prolactinoma: cabergoline 0.5mg twice weekly → highly effective (>90% normalise PRL).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Dopamine antagonists ★★ (metoclopramide, domperidone, haloperidol, risperidone, methyldopa)

content.summary: FSH >40 IU/L + amenorrhoea ≥12 months in <40y = POI — HRT essential (osteoporosis + cardiovascular protection). LH:FSH >2:1 in PCOS (60% of cases — not a diagnostic criterion). Morning testosterone (7–10 AM) essential — diurnal variation. Free testosterone (calculated) more relevant in obese (↓ SHBG → falsely low total T). Female total T >5 nmol/L → investigate virilising cause.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: LH: follicular 2–15 IU/L | FSH: follicular 3–10 IU/L | Total T: male 9.9–27.8 nmol/L, female 0.4–2.0 nmol/L

tables.caption: Causes
rows: LH + FSH ↑: POI/menopause (↑ FSH + ↑ LH + ↓ oestrogen), primary testicular failure (Klinefelter's ★), post-gonadectomy
Testosterone ↑: female PCOS ★ (mild), adrenal/ovarian tumour (very high), anabolic steroids, testicular germ cell tumour
LH + FSH ↓: hypothalamic amenorrhoea (stress, low BMI, over-exercise), hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann syndrome, pituitary failure
Testosterone ↓: primary hypogonadism (Klinefelter's), secondary hypogonadism (pituitary), obesity (↓ SHBG)

content.summary: IGF-1 is BEST screening test for acromegaly (stable throughout day, unlike GH which is pulsatile). Elevated IGF-1 → confirm with OGTT (GH measured at 0, 30, 60, 90, 120 min — normal = suppression to <1 µg/L at 2h). Acromegaly features: enlarged hands/feet, macroglossia, prognathism, carpal tunnel, sleep apnoea, HTN, T2DM, colonic polyps (3× colorectal cancer risk), organomegaly. Treatment: transsphenoidal surgery (first line) → somatostatin analogues (octreotide) if incomplete.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: IGF-1: age-dependent (see lab reference) | GH suppressed <1 µg/L post-OGTT

tables.caption: Causes
rows: IGF-1 ↑: acromegaly ★★★ (best screening test), puberty (normal), obesity (mildly elevated)
GH fails to suppress (<1 µg/L) in acromegaly on OGTT — confirmatory test
IGF-1 ↓: GH deficiency ★ (pituitary failure), malnutrition, liver disease, hypothyroidism, anorexia

content.summary: ECG of digoxin effect (not toxicity): reversed tick ST depression (scooping), short QT. Toxicity ECG: bradycardia, AV block, any arrhythmia. Treatment: stop digoxin, correct K⁺ to >4.0 mmol/L, anti-digoxin Fab (DigiFab/Digibind) for life-threatening toxicity. Narrow therapeutic index — always check K⁺ with every digoxin level. Absolutely avoid in WPW.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Toxicity ★: nausea/vomiting, yellow-green visual haloes, bradyarrhythmias, AV block, bidirectional VT
Toxicity enhanced by: hypokalaemia ★★, hypomagnesaemia, hypercalcaemia, hypothyroidism, renal failure, amiodarone, verapamil, clarithromycin (all raise digoxin levels)
Sub-therapeutic — inadequate rate control or antiarrhythmic effect

content.summary: Narrowest therapeutic window of all psychiatric drugs. Monitor pre-treatment (U&E, TFTs, ECG, pregnancy test) then every 6 months. NSAIDs are the most common cause of iatrogenic lithium toxicity — educate every patient. Dehydration (diarrhoea, vomiting, hot weather, ramadan fasting) → dangerously elevated levels. Dialysis for level >3.5 or severe neurological toxicity. Never stop lithium abruptly — taper over 4 weeks (rebound mania risk).

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Toxicity ★: tremor (first sign), GI upset, polyuria/polydipsia, confusion, ataxia, seizures, arrhythmias
Toxicity precipitants: NSAIDs ★★★ (double levels), ACEi/ARBs ★★★, thiazide diuretics, dehydration, low-sodium diet
Sub-therapeutic — inadequate mood stabilisation or prophylaxis

content.summary: AUC/MIC monitoring now preferred over trough-only (reduces toxicity). Target AUC 400–600 for serious MRSA. Monitor creatinine daily during therapy. In renal impairment: extend dosing interval (not reduce dose). High-risk nephrotoxicity: combination with pip-tazo ★ — avoid where possible or closely monitor. Check level after 3rd dose in stable renal function. Load with 25–30 mg/kg for serious infections.

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Red man syndrome: flushing/rash during infusion — slow rate (not true allergy)

content.summary: Phenytoin: non-linear kinetics — small dose ↑ causes disproportionately large level rise. In hypoalbuminaemia: corrected phenytoin = measured ÷ [(albumin g/dL × 0.1) + 0.1]. Major interactions: induces CYP450 → reduces warfarin, OCP, statins. Valproate: ABSOLUTELY contraindicated in women of childbearing potential (teratogenicity — neural tube defects + neurodevelopmental harm). MHRA: annual PPP review required for all women on valproate. Valproate-induced encephalopathy: measure ammonia — may be elevated with normal LFTs.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: Phenytoin: 40–80 µmol/L (10–20 mg/L) | Valproate: 350–700 µmol/L (50–100 mg/L) | Both: pre-dose trough

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Phenytoin toxicity: nystagmus ★ (first sign) → ataxia → dysarthria → drowsiness
Valproate toxicity: hepatotoxicity ★, hyperammonaemia (even with normal LFTs), pancreatitis, teratogenicity ★★★

content.summary: Once-daily gentamicin: 5–7 mg/kg/day (Hartford nomogram for renal dosing). Pre-dose trough MUST be <1 mg/L before next dose (drug-free interval prevents nephrotoxicity). Monitor creatinine DAILY. Stop if creatinine rises >30% from baseline. Max duration: 5–7 days where possible. Combination nephrotoxicity with: vancomycin, NSAIDs, amphotericin, furosemide — avoid or monitor closely. Endocarditis (synergistic low-dose): trough <0.5 mg/L, peak 3–5 mg/L.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: Once-daily dosing: pre-dose trough <1 mg/L | Peak (1h post): 5–10 mg/L

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Nephrotoxicity ★ (proximal tubule accumulation — monitor creatinine daily)
Ototoxicity (vestibular > cochlear — irreversible at high levels)
Sub-therapeutic — inadequate gram-negative or endocarditis coverage

content.summary: Laboratory critical values: call ordering physician within 60 minutes. Document: who notified, time, read-back of result. Patient must be reviewed within 1 hour. Do NOT simply result and walk away. Repeat sample if clinically discordant — haemolysis or sample error is common cause of spurious hyperkalaemia or thrombocytopenia.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: Values requiring IMMEDIATE physician notification and action

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Hb <60 g/L — severe anaemia → consider transfusion (threshold contextual)
Neutrophils <0.5 × 10⁹/L ★ — severe neutropenia → reverse barrier nursing, consider G-CSF
Platelets <20 × 10⁹/L — spontaneous bleeding risk → transfuse if <10 (or <20 if febrile/bleeding)

content.summary: Hyperkalaemia emergency (K >6.5): Step 1 — IV calcium gluconate 10mL 10% over 10 min (membrane stabilisation, IMMEDIATE). Step 2 — IV insulin 10U + 50mL 50% dextrose (shifts K into cells, onset 20 min). Step 3 — Salbutamol 10mg nebulised (onset 30 min). Step 4 — Resonium/patiromer (removes K, hours). Step 5 — Dialysis if refractory. ECG monitoring throughout. Identify and treat cause.

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: Values requiring IMMEDIATE physician notification and action

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Na >160 mmol/L — severe hypernatraemia — brain shrinkage, coma
K >6.5 mmol/L ★★★ — CARDIAC ARREST RISK — treat immediately
Corrected Ca >3.5 mmol/L — hypercalcaemic crisis — confusion, coma, renal failure
Creatinine >800 µmol/L — acute severe renal failure → dialysis consideration
Ammonia >150 µmol/L — hepatic encephalopathy grade III/IV
Na <120 mmol/L ★★★ — severe hyponatraemia — seizure risk — correct ≤10 mmol/L/24h (osmotic demyelination risk if faster)
K <2.5 mmol/L — severe hypokalaemia — ventricular arrhythmia
Corrected Ca <1.75 mmol/L — severe hypocalcaemia — tetany, laryngospasm, seizures
Glucose <2.5 mmol/L — neuroglycopenia — coma, permanent brain damage
Mg <0.5 mmol/L — refractory hypokalaemia, arrhythmia, respiratory failure
Phosphate <0.3 mmol/L — refeeding syndrome — haemolysis, respiratory failure

content.summary: Blood culture Gram stain guide: Gram-positive cocci clusters = MRSA/MSSA → vancomycin (MRSA) or flucloxacillin (MSSA). Gram-positive cocci chains = Streptococcus/Enterococcus → benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin. Gram-negative rods = Enterobacteriaceae or Pseudomonas → pip-tazo or cefuroxime. Yeast = Candida → echinocandin (ICU). Staph. aureus bacteraemia: ALWAYS requires echocardiogram and minimum 14 days IV antibiotics (28 days if deep focus).

alerts.label: Reference Range
alerts.text: Positive results requiring urgent clinical action and documentation

tables.caption: Causes
rows: Positive blood culture — ALWAYS call immediately — specify organism and sensitivities
CSF any organism — meningitis — antibiotics within 30 minutes of decision
MRSA in sterile site — contact precautions + notify infection control
C. difficile toxin positive — contact precautions immediately + stop offending antibiotics
AFB smear positive (TB) — respiratory isolation + public health notification
HIV positive (new diagnosis) — urgent linkage to HIV care
